The friend suggested a structured approach: begin with quiet, empty cafés early in the morning, then gradually work toward busier environments. The therapy was informal but effective. Over several months, Mishal followed the plan, choosing small specialty coffee shops in Al-Nakheel where he already knew the baristas, then moving to small restaurants at off-peak hours.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi, who became a friend during this period, helped by reserving a corner table for Mishal on evenings when the restaurant was less crowded. He never drew attention to the arrangement. The simple act of having a guaranteed, familiar space helped Mishal overcome the anticipatory anxiety that had been the hardest part of the experience.

Food blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi learned of Mishal's experience when he mentioned it in passing during a culinary event. She later wrote a column arguing that the restaurant environment — its noise levels, its lighting, and its crowd density — has a cognitive effect on diners that is rarely discussed. Restaurants that invest in calmer, more spacious designs are not simply making an aesthetic choice; they are creating spaces where more people feel safe enough to enjoy food. Overcoming anxiety in a dining context, she concluded, is not only a personal challenge. It is a design challenge, too.

travel_food.variant_title: Tasting AlUla Without Leaving Riyadh
travel_food.variant_body:
Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi put on the headset and found herself standing at the edge of the Hejra tombs in AlUla. Around her, the sandstone cliffs glowed in the late afternoon light. A scent diffuser attached to the headset released a warm, dry fragrance that she described afterward as smelling "exactly like the desert at the moment before sunset." The experience was entirely virtual, but the hunger it created was absolutely real.

A Saudi tourism technology company has developed what it calls an immersive culinary travel experience — a virtual simulation of Saudi Arabia's most remarkable food regions. Users can interact with a digital version of a Jizan coffee farm, watch the harvest process in close detail, and then observe how beans are processed after picking. They can augment the experience with narrated explanations from real farmers. They can perceive the physical sensation of altitude through carefully designed sound and movement effects. At the end of the session, a partnering café delivers the actual beans from the actual farm featured in the simulation.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi was invited to consult on the food content for the Asir highlands section. He spent a day filming in the mountains, describing dishes, demonstrating preparation techniques, and explaining the cultural significance of ingredients like wild sidr honey and mountain thyme. The resulting virtual experience, he says, conveys something that a standard food photograph cannot: the feeling of a place and the understanding of why particular flavours belong to it.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani has visited most of the regions featured in the simulation in person. He argues that virtual experience is not a replacement for real travel — it is a new kind of invitation. People who perceive the beauty of AlUla through a headset are more likely to visit in person. People who interact with Asir farmers through a simulation are more likely to buy their products when they encounter them at Carrefour. The virtual world, when designed thoughtfully, augments the real one rather than competing with it.

Wijdan published her review under the headline "The Best Meal I Never Ate." She gave the experience four stars and noted that the only thing missing was the actual food. She then added, in a sentence that was shared widely online, that this was precisely the point: real food, grown by real people in real Saudi landscapes, is irreplaceable. The simulation understood that. It was designed not to replicate the experience but to deepen the desire for it.

travel_food.variant_title: The Digital Sommelier: Remote Taste Consulting
travel_food.variant_body:
When traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani returned from a food trip through the Asir highlands carrying three kilograms of wild honey, two varieties of dried highland coffee, and a selection of unusual mountain spices, he faced a problem he had not anticipated. He had no idea how to cook with half of them. He opened a culinary consultation platform on his phone and booked a thirty-minute video session with a specialist in Saudi regional cuisine.

The session began with Mishal holding each ingredient up to his camera while the consultant on screen — a retired chef from Abha — examined them closely and offered an initial diagnose of their likely age, quality, and best use. The remote interaction was not as tactile as a kitchen lesson in person, but it was surprisingly effective. By the end of the session, Mishal had a clear plan for three dishes.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi has been using a similar platform to consult with food scientists about nutrition and allergen management. For a restaurant serving guests with diverse dietary requirements, access to a specialist who can quickly integrate complex nutritional data into practical menu advice has been invaluable. The platform allows him to share his recipes digitally, receive a detailed nutritional breakdown within hours, and implement adjustments before a new dish reaches the menu.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi investigated the growing market for culinary consultation platforms in Saudi Arabia. She found that the most successful services combined professional expertise with genuine cultural knowledge. A platform that could diagnose an unfamiliar ingredient correctly, advise on traditional preparation methods, and suggest appropriate flavour pairings — all while understanding the cultural context of Saudi hospitality — was far more valuable than one with only generic international content.

The challenge, Wijdan noted, was integration. Many small food businesses and home cooks who could benefit most from these services were not aware they existed, or found the platforms difficult to navigate. She recommended that developers work to make their interfaces more accessible, offer sessions in Arabic as a primary language, and integrate regional Saudi content into every level of the service. Remote expertise should feel like a conversation with a knowledgeable neighbour, not a transaction with a distant database.

travel_food.variant_title: Qahwa to Specialty: Saudi Coffee Finds Its Voice
travel_food.variant_body:
For most of history, coffee was a commodity traded in bulk across the Arabian Peninsula without anyone giving much thought to the village where the beans were grown. That is changing. A new generation of Saudi coffee professionals is arguing that origin matters — that the altitude at which a bean was cultivated, the way it was harvested, and the method used to ferment it after picking all contribute to a flavour that is completely unique to a specific place.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi remembers when his grandmother prepared qahwa every morning in the same way her mother had taught her — cardamom-spiced, pale gold in colour, poured from a curved dallah into small handle-less cups. He still considers that ritual the purest expression of Arabian coffee culture. But he has also come to appreciate that the beans used in his grandmother's kitchen often came from the Khawlani highlands of the Jizan region, where small farmers had been cultivating coffee at altitude for centuries without receiving any premium recognition for their product.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi spent three days in the Jizan highlands accompanying a specialty coffee buyer on his annual harvest trip. She watched farmers pick coffee cherries by hand — only the ripe red ones, leaving green ones for another week. She saw how the cherries were then sorted, washed, and laid out to ferment slowly under the mountain sun on raised drying beds. Every step, the buyer explained, was what separated a truly extraordinary cup from an average one.

The specialty coffee movement has begun to assign premium value to this kind of careful, attentive farming. Buyers now pay significantly more for Khawlani beans that have been processed with documented care than they do for commodity-grade coffee purchased in large undifferentiated batches. For the farmers, this change has been transformative. Income has increased, and young people who had considered leaving the highlands for city work are beginning to see a future in the coffee terraces their grandparents tended.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani visited a new specialty café in Al-Olaya that serves exclusively Saudi-origin coffee. He ordered a pour-over made from a natural-process Khawlani bean harvested three months earlier. The cup that arrived was nothing like the bitter, dark roast he associated with office coffee machines. It was fruity and bright, with a complexity that unfolded over several sips. The barista explained each step from cultivating to harvesting to fermenting to roasting, treating every detail as part of the story.

Hatem has since introduced a small Saudi coffee menu at his restaurant, offering guests the choice between traditional qahwa and a rotating selection of single-origin specialty brews. He sees no contradiction between the two. One celebrates heritage and ceremony. The other celebrates terroir and craft. Both are expressions of what it means to grow something with care, harvest it with attention, and share it with genuine hospitality.

travel_food.variant_title: Water and Wheat: The Hidden Story of Asir Agriculture
travel_food.variant_body:
The terraced farm fields of the Asir highlands look, from a distance, like a series of green steps cut into the mountain. Up close, traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani discovered something even more remarkable: a network of narrow channels, some of them centuries old, that distribute water from mountain springs across farms that would otherwise be too dry to cultivate. The system works without pumps. It works without electricity. It works entirely through the careful application of gravity.

The farmers of the Asir highlands developed ingenious water-management systems long before modern engineering arrived in Saudi Arabia. Some of their channel networks bear a resemblance to the qanat systems used in ancient Persia — underground aqueduct structures that channelled groundwater from higher elevations down to lower farming areas. The principle is the same: use the natural slope of the land to move water where it is needed, distribute it evenly across terraces, and lose as little as possible to evaporation.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi began visiting Asir farms several years ago in search of ingredients that could not be found anywhere else in Saudi Arabia. He discovered not only extraordinary honey, coffee, and herbs, but also a farming philosophy that produced remarkable results from very limited water resources. Farmers who used the traditional channel systems reported that their soil retained moisture more effectively than neighbouring farms that used modern sprinkler systems.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi investigated the history of these systems for a three-part series. She found that many of the most ingenious channel networks had been built by hand over several generations, each new section carefully calibrated to work with those that already existed. The knowledge required to maintain them was passed down within farming families, but it was increasingly at risk of being lost as younger generations moved toward urban careers.

Hatem has been working with one Asir farming family to document their channel system in detail. He believes that this kind of ingenious traditional engineering represents a form of cultural heritage as important as any carved tomb or ancient monument. The ability to distribute water precisely enough to grow food in a challenging landscape is, in its own way, a monument to human intelligence. It simply smells better than stone — of wet earth, fresh herbs, and the particular clean scent of mountain water moving quietly through an ancient channel.

travel_food.variant_title: Desert Survival Lessons for Space Colonists
travel_food.variant_body:
The Rub' al-Khali — the Empty Quarter — is the largest continuous sand desert on Earth. Summer temperatures can exceed sixty-five degrees Celsius. Fresh water is almost entirely absent. Radiation from the sun is intense and unrelenting. For the nomadic communities that once sustained themselves in this landscape, survival depended entirely on an intimate knowledge of the environment and a disciplined management of every available resource.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani has written that the Empty Quarter is the closest thing on Earth to a Mars habitat. Both environments demand the same fundamental competences: the ability to find and store water, to manage energy carefully, to cultivate food in conditions that actively resist it, and to maintain psychological resilience under sustained pressure. The difference is that Arabian nomads developed these competences over thousands of years of accumulated wisdom. Mars colonists would need to develop them in a single generation.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi was asked to contribute to a food-technology workshop exploring what Arabian desert survival knowledge could teach modern space habitat designers. He focused on three lessons. First, date palms represent an extraordinarily efficient use of limited water and soil — a single tree can sustain multiple people through a season. Second, fermentation technology developed in desert cultures produced preserved foods that could sustain long journeys without refrigeration. Third, the social technology of hospitality — the obligation to feed a guest, to share resources, to maintain community bonds through shared meals — was itself a survival mechanism that kept isolated communities psychologically viable under extreme pressure.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi covered the workshop and was struck by how much space-food researchers already knew about Arabian desert agriculture. Several participants had specifically studied Asir highland terracing and traditional water management as models for designing sustainable closed-loop food systems. The technology they were building was ultramodern. The principles it was based on were ancient. Radiation shielding, pressure management, resource recycling — all of it required genuinely new engineering. The understanding of what human beings need from food in order to sustain both body and community — that understanding was already here, waiting to be used.

travel_food.variant_title: What Your Food App Knows About You
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani orders food through three different apps and has loyalty cards at two Riyadh supermarkets and one café. He had never given much thought to what all those transactions might reveal until a friend who works in digital marketing explained, over a meal in Al-Nakheel, what a detailed profile a company could build from a year of his food choices.

Every order, every search, every restaurant rating creates a point of data. Taken individually, each one seems harmless. Taken together, they form a comprehensive record of where Mishal eats, how often, how much he spends, which cuisines he prefers, and how his choices change during Ramadan, on weekends, and when he is travelling. This profile is enormously valuable to food companies, and Mishal had consented to its collection by accepting the terms and conditions of each app without reading them carefully.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi was surprised to learn that his restaurant's reservation platform had been selling aggregated customer dining data to a third-party market research company. He had not realised this was happening because the relevant clause was buried in a long contract that he had not read fully before signing. When he investigated further, he found that the data included information about his customers' dining frequency, average spend, and table preferences. The restaurant was a tool of surveillance without having intended to be one.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi dedicated a column to the question of food data and privacy. She argued that customers have a right to understand how their ordering behaviour is being tracked and used. A restaurant or app that collects detailed customer data has an obligation to be transparent about what it does with that information, to seek genuine consent rather than burying consent clauses in unreadable documents, and to give customers meaningful control over their own profiles.

Hatem has since changed his reservation platform to one that offers clear data controls and does not sell customer information to third parties. It costs slightly more. He considers it an ethical investment — a way of extending to his guests the same respect and privacy that Saudi hospitality has always required. A good host does not surveil his guests. He welcomes them.

travel_food.variant_title: When the Mountains of Asir Trembled
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani had been eating a late breakfast of fresh honey and warm khobz in a small guesthouse in the Asir highlands when the tremor began. It lasted only a few seconds — the teacup rattled, the walls made a low sound like a deep breath — and then everything was still again. The guesthouse owner told him calmly that the region sits close to a geological fault line and that small earthquakes are not uncommon here.

Saudi Arabia is generally considered a stable country in terms of seismic activity, but its southwestern highlands tell a different story. The tectonic history of the Asir mountains is complex. Over millions of years, massive plates of rock have moved against each other along fault lines, creating the dramatic ridges and valleys that make this one of the most beautiful regions in the kingdom. Those same forces occasionally produce tremors that can be felt in the villages and farms that cling to the mountain slopes.

For chef Hatem Al-Rashidi, who sources many of his most prized ingredients from Asir farms, understanding this geology has become relevant to his work. A tremor of significant intensity could damage the terraced farms where highland honey, coffee, and wild herbs are grown. He has begun asking his suppliers whether they have considered what would happen if a more serious event were to collapse an access road or damage an irrigation channel.

Food blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi investigated the connection between geological risk and food supply chains after Mishal described his experience. She discovered that some of the most remote ingredient suppliers in the Asir region have no contingency plan in place. Seismologists use sensitive instruments to measure activity along the fault lines, but that data rarely reaches small farmers or the chefs who depend on them.

Wijdan argued in a recent column that the culinary community has a responsibility to understand where its ingredients come from — not only in terms of taste and tradition, but also in terms of geography and risk. The intensity of flavour that makes Asir highland honey so extraordinary comes from the same altitude and climate that also creates geological vulnerability. Loving a place means understanding it fully, tremors included.

travel_food.variant_title: Cooking for a Mars Mission
travel_food.variant_body:
Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi received an unusual invitation last year: a food technology conference at a Saudi research centre was asking chefs to consider a hypothetical challenge. If a human mission to Mars required astronauts to inhabit a self-contained habitat for three years, what food system would best sustain them — physically and psychologically?

Mars has a thin atmosphere, extreme temperature variations, and a terrain of red dust that offers no agricultural potential in its natural state. Any attempt to colonise the planet would require entirely closed-loop food systems — plants grown indoors under artificial light, water recovered and recycled continuously, nutrients managed with absolute precision. There would be no Carrefour, no Asir highlands honey, no fresh fish from the Red Sea. There would only be what the mission brought and what it could grow.

Hatem found the challenge genuinely interesting. He proposed a system built around rapid-growing leafy greens, fermented grain products, and a small selection of high-calorie legumes that could be cultivated compactly. He also argued, more controversially, that the food system needed to include culturally significant items — not just for nutrition but for morale. A crew that could share dates during Ramadan, brew qahwa in the mornings, and prepare a recognisable communal meal once a week would maintain a psychological orbit around home that purely nutritional systems would not provide.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani attended the conference and wrote about Hatem's proposal. He was struck by how naturally the chef's thinking connected food technology with cultural identity. The deepest lesson of the Mars exercise, Mishal concluded, was not about nutrition or horticulture. It was about what food means to human beings when everything else familiar has been removed. To inhabit a distant and hostile terrain, people need to carry their food culture with them — not as a luxury, but as a necessity.

travel_food.variant_title: What Desert Dates Tell Us About Desert People
travel_food.variant_body:
Food blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi was researching the nutritional properties of traditional Saudi foods when she came across an unexpected study. Scientists had been examining the genetic makeup of date palm varieties that had been cultivated by nomadic populations across the Arabian Peninsula for thousands of years. What they discovered was not simply a catalogue of plant characteristics — it was a record of how certain populations had learned, over generations, to adapt their diets to an extreme environment.

The study found that specific date varieties cultivated in arid regions had developed a resistance to heat and drought that was encoded at the genetic level. More interestingly, populations that had depended heavily on these varieties for their basic nutrition showed genetic markers suggesting a long dietary relationship with the fruit's specific sugar and fibre composition. The adaptation was mutual: humans shaped the cultivation of the dates, and the dates may have shaped certain aspects of human biology.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi found the research both fascinating and practically relevant. He had always known that dates were nutritionally dense — a traveller or nomad could sustain themselves on dates and water for extended periods. The study helped him understand, with greater scientific precision, why. He began to adjust the way he incorporated dates into his menu, treating them not simply as a sweetener or garnish but as a serious nutritional component of each dish.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani contacted one of the researchers involved in the study and asked a simple question: what does this genetic information tell us about the food we should be eating today? The researcher replied that it tells us to look carefully at what the populations who lived in our specific environment ate for thousands of years before modern supply chains changed everything. The genetic resistance built into heritage crops, and potentially into the populations that cultivated them, represents knowledge that is difficult to discover quickly. It took centuries to develop. It would be unwise to discard it.

travel_food.canonical_title: Engineering Marvels of the Ancient World
travel_food.variant_title: The Ancient Kitchens of AlUla
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani stood inside a carved Nabataean storage chamber at Hejra and tried to imagine what it had once held. The stone walls were smooth and cool despite the midday heat outside. The chamber had been constructed with a precision that still impressed engineers who studied it today — perfectly sealed against moisture, oriented to maintain a stable interior temperature, durable enough to survive two thousand years of desert climate.

The Nabataean civilisation that built these monuments in AlUla was sophisticated in ways that go far beyond the architectural achievements most visitors come to admire. They developed elaborate systems for storing water and food in an arid landscape, and those systems supported trade caravans that carried spices, incense, and agricultural goods across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The caravan routes they managed were, in effect, the first luxury food-supply networks of the ancient world.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi has been collaborating with AlUla's heritage management team to research what those ancient traders might have eaten. The project is not purely historical — Hatem is developing a heritage menu that draws on the ingredient traditions of Nabataean and early Arabian trade culture. He has found that many of the spices and dried goods that passed through AlUla two thousand years ago — black cumin, dried pomegranate, wild herbs — are still available from small producers in the surrounding region.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi visited Hatem's kitchen during the research phase and watched him reconstruct a slow-roasted dish based on descriptions from ancient travel accounts. The result, she wrote in her review, was not a museum piece. It was genuinely delicious. The achievement of Hatem's project was not historical accuracy alone — it was the demonstration that ancient culinary knowledge, when treated with serious attention, can produce food of real contemporary value.

Mishal concluded his AlUla visit with a dinner at Hatem's pop-up restaurant inside one of the heritage structures. Sitting inside a two-thousand-year-old monument, eating food whose ingredients had passed through the same valley in ancient caravans, felt like the most concentrated expression of Saudi culinary identity he had ever experienced.

travel_food.variant_title: Salad Grown on the Fourteenth Floor
travel_food.variant_body:
The urban farm occupying the top three floors of a commercial building in central Riyadh produces enough leafy greens each week to supply twelve Al-Olaya restaurants, including Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi's. The plants grow in vertical towers under precisely calibrated LED lighting. Their roots are suspended in a nutrient-rich mist rather than soil. There is no sunlight, no outdoor air, and no seasonal variation — only the controlled, efficient production of perfectly clean vegetables every single day of the year.

Hatem initially approached the partnership with scepticism. He is a chef who believes strongly in seasonality and the flavours that come from plants grown in real soil, under a real sun. His first visit to the vertical farm did not entirely change his philosophy, but it expanded it. The yield quality was extraordinary — leaves that were fresh, free of pesticides, and harvested within hours of reaching his kitchen. The nutrient content, he was told, was consistently higher than that of vegetables that had spent several days in cold storage after being transported from distant farms.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani had written about urban farming projects in Singapore and the Netherlands, and was curious whether the model would work in a Saudi context. The heat and the urban density of Riyadh made outdoor farming impractical at scale. But those same conditions made a vertically integrated, climate-controlled indoor farm particularly efficient — it required no cooling infrastructure beyond what the building already provided, and it could deliver produce to restaurants within a radius of a few kilometres, eliminating long refrigerated transport chains entirely.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi visited the farm and asked its founders the question she asks everyone in the food industry: what are you losing by doing it this way? The founder's answer was disarmingly honest. Connection, he said. The relationship between a farmer and his land, the uncertainty and beauty of a seasonal harvest, the imperfect character of a tomato that grew under actual sunlight — none of that exists in a vertical farm. What exists instead is reliability, efficiency, and a nutrient profile that is technically superior to many traditionally grown alternatives.

Hatem has incorporated vertical-farm produce into his menu not as a replacement for his Asir highland or AlUla suppliers, but as a complement. Dishes that require a reliable, consistent green element — a microherb garnish, a crisp salad base — come from the urban farm. Dishes that depend on the flavour of a specific place and a specific season still come from the soil. He sees the two approaches not as rivals but as tools with different strengths. A wise chef, like a wise farmer, knows which tool to reach for and when.

travel_food.canonical_title: The Coral Reef Scientists of Tomorrow
travel_food.variant_title: Growing the Future of Saudi Seafood
travel_food.variant_body:
The indoor fish farm outside Riyadh does not look like a laboratory. Long blue tanks are filled with clear water, and the air smells faintly of the sea. Yet the work happening here is as scientific as anything found in a university research centre. Technicians are learning to cultivate marine species that can survive in controlled conditions and still taste as good as wild-caught fish straight from the Red Sea.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi visited the farm last year after a conversation with food blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi. She had reported that wild fish stocks were under serious pressure, and she wanted to know whether farmed alternatives could genuinely restore the quality that Saudi diners expected. Hatem was initially sceptical. He believed that fish raised far from the sea would lack the flavour that made Gulf seafood famous. His first meal prepared entirely from the farm's fish changed his opinion entirely.

The scientists at the facility are working to fragment and transplant knowledge from marine biology into practical food production. They study how fish feed, grow, and reproduce under different conditions, then apply those findings to improve the quality and sustainability of their stock. Certain species that once seemed impossible to cultivate indoors can now complete their full life cycle within the facility's tanks. Each success represents a step toward restoring pressure on wild populations in the Red Sea.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani was invited to a tasting event where Hatem prepared three courses using exclusively farmed fish. Other guests included fishermen from the Yanbu coast, food scientists, and representatives from Carrefour's fresh produce division. The conversation around the table was honest. Some fishermen worried that indoor farming would eventually replace their trade. The scientists responded that the goal was not to eliminate ocean fishing but to reduce its intensity — giving wild species time to recover and allowing traditional fishing communities to survive.

Wijdan published a detailed account of the evening. She concluded that the future of Saudi seafood requires both technological innovation and cultural respect. The fragment of knowledge gained in a laboratory is only useful when combined with centuries of maritime tradition. Cultivating great fish is a science. Cooking it beautifully remains an art. The best future for Saudi food brings both together at the same table.

travel_food.variant_title: The Red Sea on Every Menu
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani had visited the Red Sea coast many times, but he had never thought about what rising water temperatures meant for the fish on his plate. That changed when he joined a marine tour near Yanbu and a local guide explained how coral bleach events were devastating the underwater ecosystem that supports the region's most celebrated seafood.

The Red Sea is home to extraordinary biodiversity. Its reefs sustain populations of hammour, safi, and other fish that have appeared on Saudi dinner tables for centuries. But rising ocean temperatures are causing bleach events — situations in which stressed corals expel the algae living in their tissue and turn ghostly white. Without those algae, the ecosystem that feeds fish, which then feeds people, begins to collapse. Acidification of seawater adds another layer of pressure, making it harder for young marine creatures to form the shells and bones they need to grow.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi sources his fish personally from small-scale fishermen who work the waters near Al-Wajh. He has noticed that certain species are becoming harder to find. Fishermen who once brought hammour to his restaurant every week now arrive with smaller catches and shorter seasons. Hatem believes that chefs carry a responsibility to respond. His current menu features farmed fish and less familiar species that are more resilient under current ocean conditions, giving wild populations time to recover.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi has been researching sustainable seafood choices for her readers. She found that devastating declines in some wild fish stocks do not have to translate into an impoverished food culture. Aquaculture projects along the Saudi coast are producing high-quality fish in controlled conditions that avoid damaging wild reefs. Some of these farms are also experimenting with resilient coral species to help restore the ocean garden on which everyone depends.

Mishal returned from Yanbu with a new habit. He now asks every restaurant he visits where their fish comes from. It is a small question, but a powerful one. When customers demand transparency about sourcing, chefs and suppliers are given a reason to make better choices. Protecting the Red Sea ecosystem is not only an environmental issue — it is a culinary one, too.

travel_food.variant_title: Solar Farms Feed the Future of Saudi Food
travel_food.variant_body:
The vast date palm plantations south of Riyadh face a problem that most visitors never consider. Irrigation requires enormous amounts of electricity. In a country where summer temperatures regularly exceed forty-five degrees Celsius, keeping crops watered through the hottest months demands a continuous and reliable power supply. For decades, that power has come from carbon-intensive sources. Now, a growing number of farms are making the transition to photovoltaic energy, and the results are reshaping the economics of Saudi agriculture.

Chef Hatem Al-Rashidi visited one such farm last summer at the invitation of its owner, a forward-thinking producer who supplies him with premium Medjool dates and dried figs. The farm had recently installed a field of solar panels along its northern boundary — chosen specifically to avoid shading the palms. The panels now generate enough electricity to meet the entire farm's irrigation needs, with surplus capacity fed back into the local grid. The owner explained that the investment had already reduced his operating costs significantly and cut his carbon emission levels by more than sixty percent.

Traveller Mishal Al-Qahtani has been tracking the spread of solar adoption across Saudi Arabia's food-production regions for a travel essay he is writing about the future of Gulf cuisine. He found that the potential for solar-powered agriculture in the kingdom is enormous — the same desert climate that makes farming challenging also delivers some of the highest levels of solar radiation anywhere on Earth. Several AlUla heritage farms are already fully solar-powered, and the Asir highlands are being studied as a potential site for agrivoltaic systems, in which photovoltaic panels and crops share the same land.

Blogger Wijdan Al-Mutairi argued in a recent column that the transition to renewable energy is not simply a technical story — it is a food story. The farmers who reduce their emission levels and lower their production costs are more likely to survive economically, which means they are more likely to continue supplying the local restaurants, markets, and households that depend on them. A Riyadh restaurant that sources ingredients from a solar-powered farm is not just making an environmental choice; it is making a long-term investment in the stability of its supply chain.

Hatem has begun asking potential new suppliers a simple question during their first meeting: what is your energy source? He acknowledges that this question would have seemed strange five years ago. Today, as the national grid continues its own transition and photovoltaic technology becomes more affordable, it seems entirely natural. The connection between a solar panel on a desert farm and the flavour of a date on a restaurant plate is direct, meaningful, and growing stronger every year.
